questionText: A man tells a woman before they marry that if she marries him, he will make her the beneficiary of his life insurance certificate. She marries him, and he later names her as beneficiary. After that, he changes the beneficiary to someone else by following the association’s procedure, and when he dies, both women claim the proceeds. Who has the better claim? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
The starting point is that, under the usual terms of life insurance (and most “associations”/group insurance certificates), the insured generally retains the right to change the beneficiary by following the procedure set out in the policy. So, purely on “who is the named beneficiary at death,” the second woman (the one named after the change) is normally the one who would receive the proceeds from the insurer.

However, a prior promise by the insured—made before marriage—can create an enforceable contract right in favor of the first woman. If there is such an enforceable right, the first woman’s claim is typically equitable (e.g., imposition of a constructive trust or equitable lien on the proceeds), even though she is not the named beneficiary at the moment of death.

1) Was there an enforceable contract/promissory obligation?
- The man’s statement (“if she marries me, I will make you the beneficiary”) is more than a vague statement of intent; it is conditional (“if she marries him”) and identifies a concrete act (naming her beneficiary).
- The woman’s marriage is the consideration for that promise: she agreed to the condition and performed it by marrying him.
- Once she marries in reliance on the promise, most courts treat the arrangement as a binding contract (or, at minimum, as enforceable under doctrines like promissory estoppel, but contract is the stronger fit if marriage was bargained-for).

2) Does the insured’s contractual duty override the policy’s revocability?
- The policy/association rules control what the insurer must do when the insured changes beneficiaries. They usually do not, by themselves, eliminate other legal obligations the insured may have had.
- A key distinction is:
  - Legal title to the proceeds normally follows the last valid beneficiary designation at death.
  - But equity can recognize that the insured breached a separate enforceable agreement to keep the first woman as beneficiary (or, at least, to secure the benefit she was promised).
- Because he initially named her, and then later removed her in violation of the promise, a court could find that the second woman holds the proceeds subject to the first woman’s equitable claim (e.g., constructive trust).

3) Who has the better claim?
- The insurer will typically pay the person who is named beneficiary at death (the second woman), because that is the contract with the insurer and the beneficiary selection followed the required procedure.
- But between the two claimants, the first woman has the stronger argument on the merits if the pre-marital promise is enforceable and covers the promised beneficiary status at the insured’s death.
  - Her claim is that she is the intended third-party beneficiary of the insured’s promise, or that the insured’s promise created an equitable interest in the policy proceeds.
  - The remedy is usually to force the proceeds (or their value) to be turned over, or to impose a constructive trust on the proceeds now held by the second woman.

Conclusion
- As to payment mechanics: the second woman is the likely “proper payee” under the policy designation at death.
- As to who has the better ultimate claim between the women: the first woman (the one promised as beneficiary in exchange for marriage, and initially named) has the better claim to the proceeds in equity, based on the insured’s enforceable promise and subsequent breach, which supports imposing an equitable remedy on whoever received the proceeds through the later designation.

If, however, the promise is treated as non-binding (e.g., merely a gratuitous statement, too indefinite, or not actually bargained-for consideration), then the second woman’s status as the named beneficiary would control and she would win. But on the facts given (“before they marry,” “if she marries him,” and then she marries and he later names her), the first woman has the stronger claim.

The woman who is named as beneficiary at the insured’s death (the second woman, to whom the insured validly changed the designation) has the better claim to the insurance proceeds. The first woman’s likely remedy is against the insured’s estate for breach of the promise—not a right to override the beneficiary change that was properly made.

1) Default rule: beneficiary at death controls, if the insured reserved the right to change
Most life insurance arrangements (including member/certificate plans administered by an association) work the same way in practice: unless the policy/certificate makes a beneficiary “irrevocable” or otherwise limits the insured’s power to change the beneficiary, the insured retains that right. If the insured changes the beneficiary in accordance with the plan’s required procedure, the new beneficiary is entitled to the proceeds at the insured’s death.

Here, the facts say the insured changed the beneficiary “by following the association’s procedure.” That strongly supports that the change was effective under the plan’s terms, so the second woman is the proper beneficiary under the certificate.

2) The pre-marriage promise can create a contract right, but it usually does not change who is entitled under the certificate
The first woman will argue that the insured promised, as an inducement to marriage, to make her beneficiary, and that she relied by marrying him—so the promise was supported by consideration and is enforceable (at least as a contract to name her beneficiary). In many jurisdictions, that is enough to give her a claim for breach.

However, the key point is that an enforceable promise generally does not automatically prevent the insured from exercising a contractual right that the insurance instrument reserves (the right to change beneficiaries). Courts often treat this as follows:

- The insured’s promise may give the promised beneficiary a claim (damages, or possibly an equitable remedy) against the insured/estate for breach.
- But it typically does not defeat an otherwise valid beneficiary designation made in accordance with the policy/plan.

So even if the first woman has an enforceable claim, the insurer generally still pays whoever is the beneficiary of record at death after a valid change.

3) When could the first woman beat the second one?
The first woman could have a stronger argument for the proceeds if she can show something like:

- the certificate/policy made her beneficiary designation irrevocable, or
- the insured entered into an agreement (with sufficient specificity and evidence) that effectively bound him not to change the beneficiary, and the court recognizes that binding agreement as creating a vested equitable interest that can attach to the proceeds (for example, via a constructive trust).

But your facts do not indicate that the beneficiary designation was irrevocable, or that there was a special agreement beyond “he will make her the beneficiary.” Without an indication that the plan terms were limited or that a binding no-change agreement is legally recognized in the jurisdiction as transferring an ownership/equitable interest in the policy proceeds, the safer/typical result is that the later beneficiary—named according to the plan’s procedure—wins the proceeds.

Conclusion
Because the insured followed the association’s procedure to change the beneficiary, the second woman (the beneficiary named at the insured’s death) has the better claim to the life insurance proceeds. The first woman’s better avenue is likely a separate claim against the insured’s estate for breach of the promise to designate her as beneficiary, rather than entitlement to override the effective beneficiary change.

As between the two women, the key issues are (1) what the life-insurance contract/bylaws allow the insured to do about changing beneficiaries, and (2) whether the insured’s pre-marriage promise created an enforceable contractual/ equitable right that prevented (or constrained) the later change.

1) Default rule under life-insurance beneficiary designations
In most life policies and fraternal/association insurance arrangements, a beneficiary designation is revocable unless the insured has made the beneficiary “irrevocable” (or otherwise has clearly waived the right to change). The insured typically retains the contractual right to change the beneficiary by following the plan’s procedures, and the beneficiary who is named at the insured’s death is generally the one entitled to the proceeds under the policy contract.

So, if the association’s procedure was correctly followed and the policy’s terms/bylaws permit changes, the association (insurer) will usually be obligated to pay the beneficiary in effect at death—here, the second woman.

2) Exception: an enforceable contract can create an equitable/contractual right
However, a beneficiary designation can become “effectively” irrevocable in equity if the insured entered into a binding contract (supported by consideration) to make that particular person the beneficiary and to ensure that the beneficiary would receive the proceeds.

Here, the man promised the woman before marriage: marry me and I will make you the beneficiary. The woman did marry him. Marriage is recognized as valid consideration, and her reliance can support enforcement of the promise as a contract (or at least as an equitable obligation).

Courts commonly treat such promises as enforceable to the extent of giving the promised beneficiary an equitable remedy if the insured later tries to defeat the bargain by changing the beneficiary. The usual result is not that the insurer breaches its contractual duties, but that the named-at-death beneficiary (the second woman) holds the proceeds subject to an equitable obligation (often described as a constructive trust) in favor of the contract beneficiary (the first woman), or the promised beneficiary can obtain damages against the insured’s estate/recipients.

3) Applying those principles to these facts
- The first woman can argue: (a) the promise was contractually enforceable (consideration: the marriage), (b) the purpose of the promise was to provide her the life-insurance security, and (c) changing the beneficiary after naming her is a breach/defeat of the bargain, so the second woman should not be allowed to keep the proceeds free of that obligation.
- The second woman can argue: (a) the policy/association bylaws allowed the change, (b) she was the properly designated beneficiary at death, and (c) absent an explicit policy waiver/irrevocable designation, the insurer’s procedure controls.

The “better claim” usually depends on whether the first woman can establish that the pre-marriage promise was more than a bare promise to “name” her at some point and instead was a promise intended to give her the proceeds upon death. Given the marriage-for-beneficiary bargain, courts are typically willing to treat the first woman as the equitable contract beneficiary.

4) Bottom-line answer
- To the association/insurer: the second woman likely has the better claim to be the proper payee, because she was the beneficiary designated at the insured’s death under the plan’s change procedures.
- As between the two women: the first woman has the stronger equitable/contractual claim to the proceeds (or their value), because the pre-marriage promise supported by the marriage consideration generally gives her an enforceable right that the insured could not defeat by later revocation. A court will commonly impose an equitable remedy (e.g., constructive trust or restitution/damages) requiring the second woman to turn over the proceeds or their equivalent.

If you tell me the jurisdiction (state/commonwealth/country) or the exact policy language (especially whether it says the beneficiary is revocable and whether irrevocability requires specific endorsement), I can tailor the analysis more precisely.

The basic rule in most life-insurance and fraternal-association “certificates” is that the insured may change the named beneficiary unless the policy/contract (or an enforceable agreement about the beneficiary) makes the beneficiary designation irrevocable. The later-named beneficiary generally wins as a matter of “who is the beneficiary of record” at the insured’s death—unless the first beneficiary can prove a legally enforceable right that prevents the change.

1) General rule: beneficiary designations are usually revocable
Even if a person initially names someone as beneficiary, the insured typically retains the right to change the beneficiary under the policy terms and under the association’s bylaws/procedure. So, standing alone, the fact that the insured previously named the woman does not establish that her interest is protected against later changes.

2) Exception: an enforceable contract can make the first beneficiary designation effectively irrevocable
A beneficiary who was promised insurance proceeds in exchange for something of value may be able to claim an equitable/vested interest if there was a contract and sufficient consideration. Here, the man promised before marriage that if she married him he would make her the beneficiary. She then married him in reliance on that promise. Marriage is recognized as valid consideration for a contract in many jurisdictions. If a court treats the promise as a binding contract (or as giving rise to an enforceable equitable right), then he cannot later defeat the promised beneficiary by changing the designation.

Courts often analyze this as an equitable interest: once the insured has bound himself by contract, a later attempt to change the beneficiary is a breach. The usual remedy is not necessarily “undoing” the association’s paperwork retroactively, but imposing a constructive trust or ordering the proceeds to be paid over to the person who has the contractual right.

3) Applying that to the facts
- She made the marriage decision because of the promise to name her beneficiary.
- He carried out the promise initially by naming her.
- He later changed the beneficiary by following the association’s procedure.

Given those facts, the first woman has a strong argument that she is not merely an “expectancy beneficiary.” She is the beneficiary of a promise made for consideration (the marriage). That contractual/equitable right is the key. If the court finds the pre-marital promise (and her performance by marrying) was enforceable, her claim is superior even though the second woman is the named beneficiary at death.

4) Practical bottom line / likely result
- If the insurance certificate/bylaws otherwise allow unrestricted beneficiary changes and there is no enforceable contract preventing a change, then the second woman (the one named at death) would have the better claim.
- On the stated facts, the better claim is generally the first woman (the promised beneficiary), because the marriage was consideration for the promise and the insured’s later change is a breach that courts can remedy by awarding/constructing an interest in the proceeds for the promised beneficiary.

Therefore, the first woman (the one promised and initially named) has the better claim, with the effect that the proceeds paid to the second woman may be subject to an order to transfer them (e.g., via constructive trust) to satisfy the first woman’s contractual/equitable right.

The starting point is that life insurance proceeds are paid to the beneficiary designated under the policy (or certificate), and in most life insurance arrangements the insured retains the right to change the beneficiary until death. If that is the governing rule of the certificate, then, as a purely “policy-law” matter, the beneficiary named at the insured’s death (the second woman) ordinarily has the superior claim to the proceeds.

However, the first woman has more than a “policy-law” claim if she can show that the insured made an enforceable promise that induced her to marry him, and that promise was breached when he later changed the beneficiary to someone else.

1) Enforceability of the promise (contract/consideration)
Before they married, the insured told the woman: marry me and I will make you the beneficiary. She married him in reliance on that promise. Marriage is valid consideration, and a promise made to induce marriage and accepted in exchange for it is typically treated as an enforceable contract term (rather than a mere gratuitous statement), assuming the proof of the promise is sufficient.

Once there is an enforceable agreement that she would be made beneficiary, equity generally does not allow the insured to take the benefit of the agreement (her marriage) and then defeat the bargain by changing the beneficiary later.

2) Effect of the insured’s right to change the beneficiary
It is true that, under the insurance certificate, the insured may have had a procedural ability to change beneficiaries. But an insured’s contractual obligation can limit how that ability is exercised. In other words, the insured may still have had the power to do the paperwork change, yet still be in breach of his agreement not to do so.

Courts commonly respond to this kind of breach not by rewriting the certificate prospectively (which is impossible once the change has been made), but by imposing an equitable remedy on the person who received the proceeds: e.g., a constructive trust or equitable lien in favor of the promisee.

3) Competing claims
- Second woman (named at death): strong legal position under the literal beneficiary designation rules of the certificate.
- First woman (induced marriage with promise): stronger equitable claim if she proves an enforceable agreement and breach—because equity treats the later beneficiary’s receipt as wrongful to the extent it violates the insured’s bargain.

Net result
If the first woman can establish that the pre-marriage promise was an enforceable contract supported by her marriage and that it obligated the insured to keep her as beneficiary (at least as against changing it to someone else), she has the better claim. The likely remedy is to order that the second woman hold the proceeds as a constructive trustee (or otherwise require payment to the first woman), despite the insured’s compliance with the insurer’s/association’s beneficiary-change procedure.

If, on the other hand, she cannot prove an enforceable obligation (for example, if the statement is treated as nonbinding, too indefinite, or unsupported by consideration beyond an unenforceable promise), then the second woman’s designation at death will control, and she will have the better claim to the proceeds.

So, on the facts given (clear promise, marriage in reliance, later designation followed by wrongful change), the first woman has the better claim in equity, even though the second woman was the named beneficiary at death.

The general rule for life insurance (including many group/association plans) is that the person who owns the policy can change the beneficiary as long as the policy’s beneficiary designation is revocable and the owner has not given up that right. Under that default rule, the woman named in the later change would normally have the “better” legal claim because she is the beneficiary on file when the insured dies, and the insurer/association typically pays the last valid designation.

However, that general rule is subject to an important exception: if the insured has made a binding contract or other arrangement that creates an enforceable, vested (equitable) right in a beneficiary, then the insured cannot defeat that right by later renaming someone else. In that situation, a court will often treat the later beneficiary as holding the proceeds subject to a constructive trust or equivalent equitable obligation in favor of the first intended beneficiary, or will award the first beneficiary the benefit she was promised.

Here, the facts support applying the exception:

1. There was a promise intended to induce marriage.  
   The man told the woman, as a condition of marriage (“if she marries him”), that he would make her the life insurance beneficiary. That is strong evidence of an agreement to confer a benefit, not a mere gratuitous hope.

2. She performed the bargained-for condition by marrying him.  
   Marriage is valid consideration in contract analysis. By marrying him in reliance on his promise, she provided the consideration contemplated by the bargain.

3. Equity can prevent the insured from changing beneficiaries in breach of the agreement.  
   Once the contract is enforceable, the insured’s later unilateral act to switch the beneficiary is typically treated as a breach that should not be allowed to defeat the promisee’s equitable interest. Courts commonly respond by imposing a constructive trust (or ordering equivalent relief) against the later named beneficiary.

Who therefore has the better claim?

- The second woman named under the association’s procedure has the better position under the default rule of “last designation controls,” assuming the policy is otherwise revocable.
- But the first woman has the better claim as a matter of enforceable contract/equitable relief, because she was induced to marry by the promise that she would be made beneficiary and she performed that condition. Her claim would typically be enforced against the proceeds now in the hands of the later beneficiary (for example, by a constructive trust), rather than being limited to damages from the insured (since the dispute is about who ultimately gets the policy proceeds).

Likely outcome: the first woman (the one promised in exchange for marriage) has the better claim, even though the association’s formal designation was changed—because her superior claim is equitable/contractual and would be enforced against the later-named beneficiary or the proceeds. 

Caveat: this can vary by jurisdiction and by the specific policy language. Some jurisdictions are stricter and require something more formal (like an irrevocable beneficiary designation, policy assignment, or an agreement that clearly waives the right to change). But on these facts—express promise, conditional on marriage, and performance—courts commonly treat the first promisee as having the stronger equitable claim.

The insurer will generally pay whoever is the named beneficiary on the policy at the insured’s death (here, the later-named beneficiary), assuming the insured retained the contractual right to change beneficiaries and followed the association’s procedure. On that “legal” level, the second woman ordinarily has the better claim to the proceeds payable by the insurer.

But the question asks which woman has the better overall claim in light of the insured’s promise made before marriage. That changes the analysis because the first woman can assert an equitable claim based on the insured’s promise.

1) Legal effect of changing the beneficiary under the policy/association rules
Most life insurance arrangements allow the policyholder to change the beneficiary unless the policy has been assigned in a way that “locks in” the beneficiary (e.g., an irrevocable beneficiary designation or an assignment that removes the right to change). If nothing indicates the first beneficiary became irrevocable, then:
- The insured’s change of beneficiary, done according to the association’s procedure, is effective as between the insured and the insurer.
- The insurer is typically discharged by paying the beneficiary named at death.
So, as to the insurer’s obligation, the second woman is usually correct.

2) Effect of the promise to make her the beneficiary
Independently of the insurer’s contractual rules, courts often treat a promise to designate a particular person as beneficiary (especially when made as consideration for marriage and relied upon) as creating an enforceable contract right or, at minimum, an equitable right in the promised beneficiary.

If the first woman proves the following (as a matter of contract/equity):
- there was a definite promise to make her the beneficiary,
- the promise was part of the consideration for the marriage (or otherwise supported by consideration),
- she relied on it (or the marriage itself serves as the bargained-for consideration in many jurisdictions),
- and the insured later intentionally breached that promise by changing the beneficiary,

then equity may prevent the insured’s later change from defeating her rights. The typical remedy is not to invalidate the insurer’s payment (because the insurer is not a party to the marital bargain), but to impose a constructive trust or equitable reimbursement obligation on whoever receives the proceeds at death (here, the second woman). In other words, the second woman may have the better “paper” entitlement from the policy, but she may hold the proceeds subject to equitable transfer to the first woman because she received them through a breach of the insured’s promise.

3) Who has the better claim?
- Second woman’s claim (named at death): strong as to entitlement against the insurer, because beneficiary changes done per the policy/association procedure are usually controlling.
- First woman’s claim (promised beneficiary): strong as an equitable claim against the proceeds received by the second woman (constructive trust / equitable reimbursement / damages against the estate, depending on the jurisdiction and the facts).

On balance, in an exam-style problem with the stated facts—“before they marry” he promised she would be made beneficiary, she married him, and then he changed the beneficiary—the first woman has the better substantive claim. The second woman likely still receives payment from the association in the first instance, but a court can require her to turn over the proceeds (or their value) to the first woman under equitable principles.

4) Important caveat
The first woman’s better claim depends on proving the promise is enforceable (not vague, not illusory, not lacking consideration) and that the insured’s change was a breach of that agreement. If the evidence is insufficient to establish an enforceable promise/contract, then the second woman’s named-beneficiary status would control, and she would be the rightful recipient.

Conclusion: The second woman is likely entitled to the proceeds as the named beneficiary at death under the policy/association procedure, but the first woman has the better overall claim because the insured’s pre-marriage promise (supported by the marriage consideration/relationship) gives rise to an equitable right—often enforced by imposing a constructive trust or equivalent relief on the later named beneficiary’s proceeds.

Generally, the person who is named as life-insurance beneficiary at the insured’s death is the person entitled to the proceeds—because the owner of a life policy normally retains the right to change the beneficiary until death, and the insurer will pay according to the last effective designation.

However, that default rule can be displaced if the insured made an enforceable contract binding himself to keep a particular beneficiary (i.e., to make the designation irrevocable). In that situation, the beneficiary who is owed performance under the contract has an equitable claim to the proceeds, and the later designation is treated as a breach of duty rather than as eliminating the earlier beneficiary’s right.

Applying that framework:

1. Legal effect of the later beneficiary change
- Following the association’s procedure and naming a new beneficiary will typically be effective under the policy’s mechanics.
- So, absent some contractual limitation, the insurer would pay the person named at death (the second woman).

2. Effect of the pre-marriage promise
- Here, the man told the woman before marriage that if she married him, he would make her the beneficiary.
- She then married him, i.e., she gave consideration (or at least detrimental reliance) in exchange for the promise.
- A court can treat that as an enforceable agreement that he would not revoke her beneficiary status (or would at minimum be obligated to see that she receives the benefit).

3. “Better claim” between the two women
- If the promise is enforceable as a contract (which is the key issue), the first woman (the wife who was promised beneficiary status) has the stronger claim—not necessarily because the second beneficiary’s designation becomes automatically void against the insurer, but because equity can impose relief on the proceeds.
- Courts commonly do this through an equitable remedy such as a constructive trust: the second beneficiary (legal payee under the final designation) holds the proceeds subject to the first beneficiary’s contractual/equitable right, or the first beneficiary can recover from the insured’s estate if the policy proceeds have been paid out.

4. When the second woman would win
- If the first woman cannot prove an enforceable contract to make her beneficiary irrevocable (for example, if the promise is too indefinite, lacks consideration, is unenforceable under the jurisdiction’s contract rules, or the policy expressly preserved a right to revoke regardless of any side promise), then the policy owner’s last designation controls and the second woman has the better claim.

Conclusion
Assuming the pre-marriage promise created an enforceable contract (made for consideration—her marriage—and binding him to keep her as beneficiary), the first wife (the one promised beneficiary status) has the better claim to the proceeds. The second wife, even though she was named at the insured’s death, would typically be treated as holding the proceeds subject to the first wife’s superior equitable/contractual right (e.g., via constructive trust or corresponding recovery).

The question turns on whether the first woman (“the promised beneficiary”) acquired any enforceable right that would prevent the insured from changing the beneficiary, or at least give her an equitable claim to the proceeds despite the later designation.

1) Default rule: a named life-insurance beneficiary usually has no vested right until the insured’s death
Most life insurance policies (including those issued by “associations”) treat the policyholder as the owner with the power to change beneficiaries. Under that default framework:
- A beneficiary designation generally creates only an expectancy while the insured is alive.
- Unless the insured has bound himself to make the beneficiary designation irrevocable (or the policy itself is written to make the beneficiary irrevocable), the insured can change the beneficiary up until death, even if the insured previously named someone else.

So, if all you had were policy mechanics, the woman the insured designated at death would ordinarily have the better claim to the proceeds.

2) But here there is an additional fact: a promise made before marriage, supported by consideration
Before they married, he promised: if she married him, “he will make her the beneficiary” of his life insurance certificate. She then married him and he designated her as beneficiary (at least initially), meaning she performed in reliance on his promise.

That typically creates an enforceable contract (or at minimum an enforceable obligation) because:
- The marriage can serve as consideration for the promise.
- The promisee (the woman) can be treated as an intended beneficiary of the insured’s performance—i.e., of the insured’s agreement to procure/designate her as beneficiary.

3) Effect of that contract on the ability to change the beneficiary
Even if the policy allows the insured to change beneficiaries, many courts will not allow the insured to defeat the bargained-for arrangement by simply revoking later. The insured’s later change is usually treated as a breach of the promise, and equity often intervenes to prevent unjust enrichment.

Common results in disputes between two claimants are:
- The later-named beneficiary may be paid by the insurer (because insurers generally pay the beneficiary on the face of the policy), but
- The later beneficiary may take the proceeds subject to an equitable remedy—such as a constructive trust or equitable lien—in favor of the promised beneficiary, reflecting the insured’s contractual obligation.

4) Who has the better claim between the two women?
On these facts, the first woman has the stronger underlying claim, because:
- She has the contractual/reliance theory: the insured induced her marriage by promising the life-insurance beneficiary designation.
- He initially designated her, consistent with that promise.
- He then breached the promise by changing the beneficiary in accordance with the procedure, but that procedural compliance does not erase the insured’s separate contractual commitment.

Accordingly, the second-named beneficiary’s claim is weaker in equity: her designation at death may govern payment mechanics, but the first woman is the one with the better claim to ultimate entitlement via enforcement of the promise (typically through an equitable constructive trust or similar relief).

Bottom line: the promised beneficiary (the first woman) has the better claim to the proceeds (or, at minimum, a superior equitable right to impose a constructive trust/equitable lien on them) because the insured’s pre-marriage promise, supported by the marriage consideration and reliance, creates an enforceable obligation that the later beneficiary cannot defeat by a beneficiary-change procedure.

As a starting point, the insured generally has the right to change a life-insurance beneficiary unless the insured has bound himself not to do so. Most life-insurance policies (and many fraternal/association certificates) treat the beneficiary designation as revocable at the insured’s discretion, and beneficiary status ordinarily vests only when the insured’s right to change has been effectively limited (e.g., by an irrevocable beneficiary designation or a valid contract that makes the beneficiary’s interest enforceable).

Here, the insured did two key things:

1) Before marriage, he told the woman that if she married him he would make her the beneficiary.  
2) After marriage, he indeed named her beneficiary, but later he changed the beneficiary using the association’s required procedure.

The second woman is the named beneficiary at death, so she has the strongest “paper”/legal claim under the certificate’s beneficiary-change mechanism. Following the association’s procedure typically controls who is entitled to the proceeds as the matter of the association’s administration of the certificate.

The first woman’s claim, however, is that the man made an enforceable promise to her, supported by consideration (the marriage), and therefore he should not be allowed to defeat her equitable interest by changing the beneficiary later.

The central legal question becomes whether the promise before marriage created an enforceable contractual/equitable right to the proceeds that the insured could not defeat by later complying with the association’s change procedure.

Why the first woman likely has the better claim (equitable priority)
- Consideration: Her marriage is a classic form of bargained-for consideration. If the promise (“marry me and I will make you the beneficiary”) was sufficiently definite, a court can treat it as a contract.
- Enforceability as to beneficiary changes: Once the insured has entered a contract obligating himself to keep her as beneficiary (or to ensure that she ultimately receives the proceeds), equity generally treats him as having created a beneficial interest in her that cannot be nullified by later revocation.
- Practical remedy: Even though the association will often pay the proceeds to the person listed at death, courts commonly impose a constructive trust (or allow reimbursement) to prevent the second beneficiary from being unjustly enriched where the insured breached a duty to the promised beneficiary.

So, although the association properly paid (or would pay) the second beneficiary because the insured followed the change procedure, the first woman can argue that the second beneficiary holds the proceeds subject to the first woman’s equitable right arising from the insured’s promise.

Why the association-compliance fact does not automatically decide it
The fact that the insured followed the association’s procedure normally determines who is the “designated” beneficiary. It does not necessarily resolve who has the better substantive claim where there is an independent contract right (or fraud/unjust enrichment theory) that limits the insured’s ability to change the beneficiary’s ultimate entitlement.

Possible limiting counterarguments (when the second beneficiary might win)
The second woman’s best defense would be that, absent an irrevocable designation or strict compliance with any bylaw/policy method for creating an irrevocable beneficiary, the first woman’s interest never vested and she should have only an action for breach of promise/damages against the insured’s estate—not direct entitlement to the insurance proceeds. Some jurisdictions treat fraternal/association certificates as heavily controlled by bylaws, meaning a beneficiary contract might entitle the promised beneficiary to damages rather than to the proceeds themselves.

But on the facts given (a clear promise tied to marriage, followed by naming and then changing beneficiaries), the typical legal approach is that the promised beneficiary has the superior equitable claim and can seek imposition of a constructive trust or reimbursement over the proceeds.

Conclusion
The second woman has the better claim as the named beneficiary under the certificate at death. However, the first woman has the better substantive/equitable claim because her marriage was consideration for the insured’s promise to make her beneficiary, and the insured should not be allowed to defeat that promise by later revoking her through the association’s procedure. The usual result is that the second woman’s receipt is subject to the first woman’s equitable right (often enforced through a constructive trust or reimbursement/damages from the proceeds, depending on the governing association/bylaw rules and jurisdiction).

# Legal Analysis of Life Insurance Beneficiary Claims

The woman he married has the better claim to the life insurance proceeds, though the analysis depends on several factors including the nature of the promise, applicable law, and the circumstances of the beneficiary change.

## The Promise as a Contract for Consideration

The husband's pre-marital promise to make his wife a beneficiary constitutes a binding contract if the wife's marriage constitutes consideration. Courts generally recognize that marriage itself can constitute valid consideration for a promise. Here, the wife married in reliance on his promise, establishing mutual consideration: he received the marriage he desired, and she received the promise regarding the insurance beneficiary designation.

## The Actual Designation and Its Legal Effect

When the husband subsequently named her as beneficiary and she became the designated beneficiary on the certificate, her position strengthened considerably. Most jurisdictions recognize that once a beneficiary designation is made and communicated, the beneficiary acquires a vested interest in the proceeds. This is distinct from a mere contractual promise—it represents an actual legal status under the insurance contract.

## The Subsequent Change of Beneficiary

The critical issue is whether the husband had the legal right to unilaterally change the beneficiary after naming his wife. # Legal Analysis of Competing Beneficiary Claims

## The Controlling Principle

The woman who is named as beneficiary at the time of the insured's death has the superior legal claim to the insurance proceeds. This principle derives from the fundamental nature of life insurance beneficiary designations as revocable contractual arrangements between the policyholder and the insurance company.

## Why the Current Beneficiary Prevails

**Revocability of Beneficiary Designations**

Life insurance policies, unless specifically made irrevocable, permit the policyholder to change the beneficiary at any time during his lifetime. This is a well-established principle in insurance law. The right to change beneficiaries is typically a reserved power of the policyholder and cannot be restricted by prior promises or agreements unless the beneficiary designation itself is made irrevocable through specific contractual language or statutory requirement.

**The Promise as Contract**

The initial promise to make the first woman beneficiary, even if made before marriage as an inducement to marry, is separate from the actual beneficiary designation. While such a promise might constitute a valid contract between the man and the first woman—potentially giving her a contractual claim against his estate for breach—it does not create an irrevocable beneficiary designation in the insurance policy itself.

**The Designation Controls the Proceeds**

Once the man properly changed the beneficiary by following the insurance association's procedures, that change became effective. The insurance company's obligation is to pay the proceeds according to the designation in effect at death. The second beneficiary's claim is direct and absolute—she is the named beneficiary in the policy document itself.

## The First Woman's Potential Remedy

The first woman's claim is not entirely without merit, but it lies in a different direction. She could potentially:

1. **Sue the estate for breach of contract** if she can establish that the promise to name her as beneficiary was a binding contract made as consideration for her marrying him. She would seek damages from the estate, though this would not give her the insurance proceeds directly.

2. **Claim promissory estoppel** in some jurisdictions, arguing that she relied to her detriment on his promise by marrying him. However, this remedy typically results in equitable relief rather than direct recovery of the insurance proceeds.

3. **Challenge the change of beneficiary** if she can demonstrate that the policyholder lacked capacity, was subject to undue influence, or that the change violated an irrevocable beneficiary agreement—a high burden to meet.

## Conclusion

The second beneficiary (the one named at death) has the substantially better claim to the insurance proceeds. Life insurance proceeds are distributed according to the designation in effect at the insured's death, not according to prior promises or agreements. The first woman's remedy, if any, would be a contractual claim against the estate, not a claim to the insurance proceeds themselves. The insurance company would be obligated to pay the named beneficiary at death, and the first woman's dispute is with the insured's estate, not with the insurance company or the proceeds.

The starting point is that, under the usual terms of life insurance (and most “associations”/group insurance certificates), the insured generally retains the right to change the beneficiary by following the procedure set out in the policy. So, purely on “who is the named beneficiary at death,” the second woman (the one named after the change) is normally the one who would receive the proceeds from the insurer.

However, a prior promise by the insured—made before marriage—can create an enforceable contract right in favor of the first woman. If there is such an enforceable right, the first woman’s claim is typically equitable (e.g., imposition of a constructive trust or equitable lien on the proceeds), even though she is not the named beneficiary at the moment of death.

1) Was there an enforceable contract/promissory obligation?
- The man’s statement (“if she marries me, I will make you the beneficiary”) is more than a vague statement of intent; it is conditional (“if she marries him”) and identifies a concrete act (naming her beneficiary).
- The woman’s marriage is the consideration for that promise: she agreed to the condition and performed it by marrying him.
- Once she marries in reliance on the promise, most courts treat the arrangement as a binding contract (or, at minimum, as enforceable under doctrines like promissory estoppel, but contract is the stronger fit if marriage was bargained-for).

2) Does the insured’s contractual duty override the policy’s revocability?
- The policy/association rules control what the insurer must do when the insured changes beneficiaries. They usually do not, by themselves, eliminate other legal obligations the insured may have had.
- A key distinction is:
  - Legal title to the proceeds normally follows the last valid beneficiary designation at death.
  - But equity can recognize that the insured breached a separate enforceable agreement to keep the first woman as beneficiary (or, at least, to secure the benefit she was promised).
- Because he initially named her, and then later removed her in violation of the promise, a court could find that the second woman holds the proceeds subject to the first woman’s equitable claim (e.g., constructive trust).

3) Who has the better claim?
- The insurer will typically pay the person who is named beneficiary at death (the second woman), because that is the contract with the insurer and the beneficiary selection followed the required procedure.
- But between the two claimants, the first woman has the stronger argument on the merits if the pre-marital promise is enforceable and covers the promised beneficiary status at the insured’s death.
  - Her claim is that she is the intended third-party beneficiary of the insured’s promise, or that the insured’s promise created an equitable interest in the policy proceeds.
  - The remedy is usually to force the proceeds (or their value) to be turned over, or to impose a constructive trust on the proceeds now held by the second woman.

Conclusion
- As to payment mechanics: the second woman is the likely “proper payee” under the policy designation at death.
- As to who has the better ultimate claim between the women: the first woman (the one promised as beneficiary in exchange for marriage, and initially named) has the better claim to the proceeds in equity, based on the insured’s enforceable promise and subsequent breach, which supports imposing an equitable remedy on whoever received the proceeds through the later designation.

If, however, the promise is treated as non-binding (e.g., merely a gratuitous statement, too indefinite, or not actually bargained-for consideration), then the second woman’s status as the named beneficiary would control and she would win. But on the facts given (“before they marry,” “if she marries him,” and then she marries and he later names her), the first woman has the stronger claim.

The first wife has the superior claim to the life insurance proceeds. While life insurance policies typically grant the insured the right to change beneficiaries at will, this right is subject to any valid, binding contractual obligations the insured has previously entered into regarding the policy.

In this scenario, the man made a specific promise to name his wife as the beneficiary in exchange for her entering into the marriage. Under the law of contracts, marriage constitutes valid and sufficient consideration. When the woman married him in reliance on that promise, she performed her part of the bargain, thereby acquiring a vested equitable interest in the insurance proceeds. 

Although the man followed the association’s formal procedures to change the beneficiary, he lacked the legal right to do so because he had already encumbered the policy through his prior contract. While the insurance association may be protected from liability by following its internal procedures for changing a beneficiary, the dispute between two competing claimants is governed by equitable principles rather than just the association’s administrative rules.

Courts generally hold that when an insured enters into a binding contract to name a specific person as a beneficiary, that person acquires an equitable interest that cannot be defeated by a subsequent change of beneficiary to a volunteer—someone who has not provided value for the designation. Because the second beneficiary is typically a gratuitous recipient (a "volunteer") and did not provide consideration for being named, her claim is subordinate to the first wife’s vested equitable right. Therefore, the first wife is entitled to the proceeds, as equity regards as done that which ought to have been done, and the husband’s attempt to name a new beneficiary constituted a breach of his prior contractual obligation.
